documents.title: Portfolio assistant FAQ
documents.content:
## What is this portfolio trying to show?

The site is meant to show how Francisc frames ambiguous work, translates it into structured artifacts, and delivers systems that hold up after handover.

## What kinds of roles does the portfolio support?

The material is strongest for technical program management, product or platform roles with systems responsibility, workflow-heavy AI adoption work, documentation-heavy platform work, and security-aware web platform work.

## What should a reader look at first?

If the reader wants delivery evidence, start with the work case studies. If they want thought process and trade-off framing, start with the writing. If they want role-fit evidence, use the resume page and role-specific resumes.

## What should the assistant avoid claiming?

The assistant should avoid claims that are not directly supported by public material, including invented metrics, speculative role claims, or private project details.

documents.title: About
documents.content:
I work across product management, UX, WordPress engineering, and platform security. Nine years in, I've come to believe that clear requirements matter as much as the code itself.

## What I'm best at

- Turning ambiguous ideas into clear requirements, user stories, and acceptance criteria.
- Bridging business goals, design intent, and technical constraints before build starts.
- Shipping systems with handover docs and runbooks so the next person can actually operate them.

## Evidence from recent work

- Built and maintained 60+ web products for SMEs, research teams, and SaaS contexts.
- Cut downtime and attack surface on client environments by up to 70% through CDN, firewall, and hardening work.
- Improved engagement by 40% on educational research interfaces through UX redesign and iterative validation.

## How I work

1. Discovery: define the problem, users, constraints, and non-goals.
2. Requirements: write stories, acceptance criteria, and decision rationale.
3. Delivery: balance UX quality with platform, security, and performance constraints.
4. Handover: leave documentation thorough enough that teams can run and extend the system without me.

## Core stack

WordPress, WooCommerce, Elementor, PHP, JavaScript, Python, REST APIs, MySQL, NGINX, Redis, Cloudflare, AWS, and workflow documentation practices.

## Current direction

Focusing on Technical Product Manager, Product/Platform Engineer, UX Systems, and Security-aware Web Platform roles.

## Contact

Email [francisc.frd@gmail.com](mailto:francisc.frd@gmail.com) for roles and collaborations, or use the form.

documents.title: Now
documents.content:
Updated: January 2026

## Current focus

- Designing clearer case-study formats for product and engineering work.
- Tightening documentation workflows so onboarding takes days, not weeks.
- Building lightweight automation for repeat delivery tasks.

## Learning

- Measurable UX outcomes without heavy analytics overhead.
- Security-by-default practices for frontend-heavy systems.

## Open to

- Product engineering roles with real ownership.
- Platform UX and documentation-heavy system design.

documents.title: Resume
documents.content:
## Summary

Product-oriented technologist with 9+ years of experience working across business requirements, UX decisions, and technical delivery. I build clarity in complex environments through strong documentation, explicit scope, and durable implementation patterns.

## Highlights

- 9+ years across product, UX, web engineering, and systems/security operations.
- 60+ websites and platform implementations delivered for SMEs and research organizations.
- Technical lead on a secure accounting integration platform (Sage50 context).
- Hands-on infrastructure and security work across Cloudflare, AWS, Linux, and monitoring pipelines.

## Experience timeline

### Product & Technical Consultant / Founder

`SYNQ Group LLC` · Boston, MA · 2020 — Present

- Act as the bridge between stakeholders, designers, and engineers.
- Define problem statements, user stories, acceptance criteria, and delivery constraints.
- Deliver handover documentation: system overviews, setup guides, and maintenance instructions.
- Architect and maintain WordPress environments focused on performance, reliability, and security.

### Digital Experience Designer / Technical Generalist

`Independent` · Dublin, Ireland · 2016 — 2020

- Designed and delivered 60+ web products across multiple industries.
- Built IA, user flows, and UX decisions aligned with technical constraints.
- Implemented conversion and analytics instrumentation (Hotjar, Clarity, GA/GTM) and iterative UX improvements.

### Technical Lead

`Suntico Holdings` · Dublin, Ireland · 2014 — 2017

- Led delivery for a secure, web-based accounting integration platform.
- Coordinated engineering, compliance, and business requirements.
- Defined internal API usage, access models, and governance documentation.

## Skills snapshot

| Area | Tools / capabilities |
| --- | --- |
| Product execution | Requirements definition, user stories, acceptance criteria, trade-off analysis |
| UX and design | Information architecture, wireframing, usability testing, stakeholder workshops |
| WordPress engineering | WooCommerce, Elementor, ACF, custom integrations, migration and scaling |
| Infrastructure and security | Cloudflare WAF, SSL/TLS, DNS, NGINX, Redis, MySQL, vulnerability and incident workflows |
| Automation and tooling | Bash, Python, JavaScript, Git, monitoring and reporting automation |

## Education

`BEng/BSc in Enterprise Computing` · Dublin City University · 2024

Relevant coursework: Software Engineering, Enterprise Systems, System Integration, Cloud Platforms, Data Security, Human-Computer Interaction.

## Certifications

In progress: CompTIA Security+, ISC2 Certified in Cybersecurity.

## Download role-specific resumes

- [Technical Product Manager Resume](/resumes/francisc-furdui-technical-product-manager.pdf)
- [Cybersecurity Analyst Resume](/resumes/francisc-furdui-cybersecurity-analyst.pdf)
- [UI/UX Resume](/resumes/francisc-furdui-ui-ux.pdf)
- [WordPress Developer Resume](/resumes/francisc-furdui-wordpress-developer.pdf)
- [General Resume](/resumes/francisc-furdui-general.pdf)

documents.title: Portfolio positioning
documents.content:
Francisc Furdui is positioning for roles that sit between product judgment, systems thinking, and technical delivery.

The strongest through-line in the portfolio is not a single tool or title. It is the ability to take ambiguous work, make the constraints explicit, and leave behind artifacts that help a team operate after launch.

The material supports a few recurring claims:

- He works well in workflow-heavy and cross-functional environments.
- He thinks about AI adoption as an operational systems problem, not a demo problem.
- He values documentation, handover quality, and run-state reliability as part of delivery quality.
- He can connect product framing, UX structure, and implementation constraints.

The portfolio does not try to overstate narrow specialization. It suggests breadth with a systems bias, especially around documentation, governance, platform operations, and AI-enabled work.

documents.title: Profile summary
documents.content:
# Profile summary

Name: Francisc Furdui
Role: Technical Program Manager focused on AI adoption systems
Location: Boston

## Positioning

What interests me is the part after the demo. How does a team introduce a new capability, keep context intact, and turn one-off experiments into something people can actually use? That usually leads me to workflow design, knowledge structure, and the operational details that keep a system from drifting.

## Focus

- AI adoption in enterprises
- agent architectures
- technical program management
- knowledge systems
- workflow automation
- governance and auditability

## Capability signals

- AI Adoption Systems: I build the structure around adoption: rollout paths, ownership, and the guardrails that keep a new tool useful after the first week. Evidence: Current work.
- Agent Infrastructure: I care about the layer behind the interface: how agents get context, use tools, and fit inside the constraints of a real operating environment. Evidence: SYNQ Engine.
- Knowledge Systems: Good automation depends on clean context. I design the docs, content models, and decision trails that make systems easier to trust and extend. Evidence: Requirements system.
- Workflow Automation: A lot of coordination work is still manual. I look for the parts that can be made repeatable without making the process brittle. Evidence: Security resilience.

## Selected work

- SYNQ Engine: Agent orchestration runtime built for WordPress-heavy environments. (Live system)
- STARTLINE: Working framework for enterprise AI adoption and workflow change. (Current work)
- Cycl: Current exploration in AI-assisted credit planning and optimization. (Current work)
- Requirements and handover documentation system: Documentation framework for scoping work, making trade-offs explicit, and handing systems over cleanly. (Documented case study)
- WordPress migration and platform modernization: Migration playbook focused on performance, governance, and cleaner long-term maintenance. (Documented case study)
- Security hardening and incident resilience: Security and monitoring routines designed to reduce avoidable incidents and speed up response. (Documented case study)

documents.title: Requirements and handover documentation system
documents.content:
## Problem / Context

Projects stalled because teams had partial context. Business goals were clear in the kickoff meeting, then vague by the time engineering started.

## Approach

I made discovery and handover documentation part of delivery, not cleanup you do at the end.

- Define the problem and success criteria before implementation.
- Write user stories and acceptance criteria in plain language.
- Capture decision rationale when trade-offs are made.

## Execution

- Created reusable templates for discovery, scope, and handover.
- Linked technical decisions to business and UX goals.
- Produced maintenance instructions for teams inheriting systems after launch.

```ts
type DeliveryArtifact = {
  problem: string;
  acceptanceCriteria: string[];
  constraints: string[];
  handoverChecklist: string[];
};
```

## Results

- Teams aligned earlier and reduced avoidable rework.
- Stakeholders had clearer ownership boundaries during implementation.
- Non-technical operators could manage production systems with less dependency on original builders.

## Learnings

Documentation quality directly affects delivery speed and long-term maintainability.

## Artifacts

- Discovery and scope template set
- Handover and maintenance guides
- Decision record format

documents.title: Educational research UX redesign
documents.content:
## Problem / Context

Educational and research organizations needed sites that made complex topics legible to non-technical audiences — and could be maintained by those same people after handover.

## Approach

I treated each redesign as a UX and requirements problem, not just a visual one.

- Mapped user journeys before visual design.
- Clarified page goals and conversion intent per section.
- Used lightweight usability checks to validate decisions before launch.

## Execution

- Built wireframes and prototypes in Figma.
- Implemented production builds in WordPress.
- Added analytics and behavior tools to monitor drop-offs and navigation friction.
- Iterated content hierarchy and CTA order based on observed usage.

## Results

- Engagement improved by 40% on key project surfaces.
- Users reached high-intent content faster with fewer navigation loops.
- Stakeholders gained a reusable pattern for future content additions.

## Learnings

Clear information architecture does more for trust and usability than visual polish alone.

## Artifacts

- User journeys and IA map
- Wireframes and interaction prototypes
- Post-launch behavior review notes

documents.title: WordPress migration and platform modernization
documents.content:
## Problem / Context

Organizations on older CMS setups or page-builder platforms were paying a maintenance tax: slow deploys, brittle plugins, and no clear owner when something broke.

## Approach

I scoped migration work as a full system transition, not a template swap.

- Audit content, URL structure, and integrations.
- Define migration acceptance criteria (performance, parity, SEO safety).
- Build a repeatable rollout and rollback process.

## Execution

- Rebuilt site architecture on WordPress with modern infrastructure.
- Tuned caching and database layers with NGINX, Redis, and MySQL optimization.
- Integrated CDN and security controls via Cloudflare.
- Delivered post-launch documentation for updates and incident handling.

## Results

- Faster, more stable websites across migrated properties.
- Lower incident frequency and improved operational consistency.
- Clearer ownership for ongoing content and technical maintenance.

## Learnings

Migration success depends on disciplined requirements and rollback planning, not just implementation speed.

## Artifacts

- Migration checklist and launch runbook
- Performance baseline + post-launch comparison
- Maintenance and governance notes
documents.excerpt: ## Problem / Context Organizations on older CMS setups or page-builder platforms were paying a maintenance tax: slow deploys, brittle plugins, and no clear owner when something broke. ## Approach I scoped migration wo...

documents.title: Security hardening and incident resilience
documents.content:
## Problem / Context

Small and mid-sized organizations often had the right tools but no operational model behind them. Alerts fired, patches waited, and incidents had no clear owner.

## Approach

I focused on pragmatic controls tied to measurable operational outcomes.

- Strengthen perimeter and access controls.
- Monitor critical events and certificate/uptime health.
- Define incident triage and post-incident hardening steps.

## Execution

- Deployed Cloudflare firewall and policy controls.
- Implemented SSL, DNS, and access hardening patterns.
- Set up vulnerability scanning and log review workflows.
- Automated recurring maintenance tasks for patching and monitoring.

```yaml
security_baseline:
  - waf_rules_enabled
  - ssl_tls_enforced
  - uptime_and_log_alerts
  - vulnerability_scan_cycle
```

## Results

- Reduced downtime and attack impact by up to 70% in targeted environments.
- Better visibility into risk through monitoring and reporting routines.
- Faster incident containment with clearer ownership and response playbooks.

## Learnings

Security improvements hold only when controls are paired with consistent operational habits.

## Artifacts

- Security baseline checklist
- Incident response and post-mortem template
- Vulnerability remediation workflow

documents.title: Bridging UX and engineering
documents.content:
## What breaks the bridge

In mixed product/design/engineering teams, the most expensive failures usually happen before coding starts. The brief is often directionally right but operationally vague.

## What fixes it

A short alignment pass can prevent weeks of rework. I use this sequence:

1. Define user objective and business objective separately.
2. Document constraints (time, platform, security, ownership).
3. Convert UX intent into testable acceptance criteria.
4. Log trade-offs as soon as they are made.

  If the team cannot explain the non-goals in one minute, the scope is still unstable.

## Why this matters

Working across UX, WordPress engineering, and product management, I kept coming back to the same problem: intent evaporates between conversations. The bridge is not a meeting. It's a shared artifact.

documents.title: Systems thinking in web projects
documents.content:
## A website is an operating system

Across 60+ deliveries, the same pattern: teams treat launch as the finish line, then struggle in maintenance. Real quality shows up after handover.

## Design for run-state, not demo-state

Before implementation, I now define:

- who updates content,
- who owns hosting and security,
- what gets monitored,
- what happens when something breaks.

This changes technical decisions early. For example, component and CMS choices become easier when operational constraints are explicit.

## Connect UX and infrastructure

Users feel slow pages, broken forms, expired SSL, and confusing navigation as one problem. They do not separate design from systems.

That is why delivery plans should combine:

1. information architecture,
2. performance baselines,
3. security controls,
4. handover documentation.

## Practical rule

If your project plan has no maintenance section, it is not a project plan. It is a launch checklist.
